title: vimeo_1202331814
displayTitle: 2026-06-17 - Justice Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Justice
description: Thursday 18 June 2026 | Justice Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Justice Hearing of Evidence Submitter: Hon Nicole McKee, Associate Minister of Justice Hon Karen Chhour, Minister for the Prevention of Family and Sexual Violence, Minister for Children
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Justice. Hon Nicole McKee, Associate Minister of Justice. Hon Karen Chhour, Minister for the Prevention of Family and Sexual Violence, Minister for Children

chunks.text: Very nice to have you here with us, Minister. Now, we've got 45 minutes with you, just to be clear. And obviously you introduced your team, some of whom we've seen many times before. I think we've seen everyone, virtually. But anyway, the floor is yours. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I think you do know the team that are with me, so I won't go through introductions except to say that we have Ministry of Justice, including a good part of the firearms team that are here with us today. But I'll just have some brief introductory remarks. Mr. Chair, I appreciate the

opportunity to appear before the committee today as Associate Minister of Justice, and I will keep my opening remarks brief just for the benefit of the Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. Oh, that's a... Oh. The Associate Justice portfolio covers a range of areas including firearms, alcohol, adoption, anti-money laundering, and more. Across all these distinct areas, it is necessary to protect public safety while also ensuring that law-abiding New Zealanders can go about their business and enjoy their lives without unnecessary red tape, cost, and paperwork.

Regulation needs to be practical, proportionate, and workable for the people and businesses who follow the rules. On firearms, we are reforming the law, regulations, and institutions to improve public safety while treating licensed firearm owners fairly. Budget 2026 provides $44.9 million over four years to deliver the Arms Act rewrite so that we have a firearm system that improves public safety, trust, and provides efficient, effective, and responsive regulatory services to licensed firearm owners.

We are also overhauling New Zealand's anti-money laundering and countering financing of terrorism system to make it easier to do business and harder to do crime. The government has also taken decisive action on overseas adoptions. Last year, we moved quickly with urgent legislation to reduce the risk of harm to some children and young people adopted overseas. In May, the government introduced the Overseas Adoptions Legislation Bill to replace the temporary changes that are due to expire on 1 July 2027. And finally, our

alcohol reform package is focused on practical regulation. The goal is to make the licensing process fairer, make it easier to host events, and cut through red tape affecting Kiwi businesses without increasing the risk of alcohol-related harm. Across this portfolio, the theme is consistent, Mr. Chair. We're protecting the public from real harm, backing legitimate users and businesses, and cutting red tape where it is safe to do so. I'm now open to take questions.

What a perfectly timed introduction. Right, I'm going to turn to Honorable Ginny Andersen. Thank you very much. Thank you for your introductory remarks, Minister. I've had a couple of emails through from members of the public who are firearm owners who have raised the same issue, and it's a new type of firearm that's been available called a push-button rifle.

And you might know more than I, but it's described as not quite a semiautomatic, but very close. It has a button on the receiver. It has to be pushed to load another cartridge. It cycles slower than, say, an AR-15, but faster than any other type of repeating gun and is designed so firing can take place very rapidly and comes in a .223 caliber.

There's a concern being raised that there's a weakness in our laws, that these aren't covered by the current legislation. Do you have a view on whether they should be prohibited or where they should sit in terms of their capability? Thank you. Thanks to the member for her question, because there is some information out there that I don't think is quite right on the push-button firearms. For example, it's not a new type of action or a new type of firearm. It's been around for quite some substantial period of time. It has been approved by New Zealand Police to be

within the country. It is nothing like a semiautomatic at all. It can run either on gas or on inertia. And a semiautomatic is where a firearm will, once you pull a trigger, it will fire, it will eject, and it will reload a new round ready for the person or the operator to just pull the trigger again. A push-button firearm does not work like that.

When the trigger has been pulled, a round will expel, the action will open, a round will pop out, but the action is actually held back, or the bolt is held back. So it's not-- A semiautomatic is where you go through the entire process, all of the steps all at once. This does not occur with a push button.

In order to load the next round, to have the chamber locked, you need to push that button, and that actually requires a person to remove their finger to enable that to happen, depending on the type. Yeah. And it is nothing at all like the semiautomatics. So I think that that's misinformation that might be getting out there by people that it's similar to. I don't believe that it is.

One would argue that a lever action could be seen as that as well, and that there are other types of firearms that can be seen like that as well. But I think what we can-... come down to, as they have been around for a very long time. Okay, let's move to the budget now. Okay. The $4.7 million of funding for the new Firearms Licensing Review Committee, what date do you expect to appoint the individuals to these roles?

We would like to see that the Firearms Licensing Review Committee is stood up and ready to go as soon as the changeover occurs. We will be advertising shortly for those positions. In fact, I think they're underway now, the advertisements for the licensing review committee, in order to make sure that we have the right people in place ready to go from day one.

Is it likely to be a minister yourself, or is it Minister Goldsmith who'll be doing the appointing of those roles? Well, the interviews for those roles will actually occur under the Ministry of Justice, and the minister in charge of firearms will be the person that appoints those roles. So it will actually go through a process that the ministry will undertake, and they will deliver, as they usually do, recommendations to the minister to make the final decisions.

What checks and balances are there for the new firearms regime in terms of appointments? So will the Public Service Commission play any sort of oversight role with the new chief exec of the organization, understanding it's a statutory appointment and not core public service? Can you just give us a bit more information on how that works? I'm actually going to pass that over.

Because of the serious accusations that have been personally made against me in regard to appointments within this realm, I've actually handed the entire lot over to the Ministry of Justice, so perhaps they could answer that question for you. So I can talk to that. Clearly this committee has considered some of the submissions that came in on the firearms legislation in terms of ensuring an appropriately independent appointment process, and there are particular responsibilities given me.

They've been designed to, as closely as possible, follow the public services approach. The reason that they are with the Ministry of Justice rather than the public service is that police is not a public service department, and so we'd had to recreate that. I've run the process that's underway at the moment in terms of identifying a chief executive for the new agency.

We have been very deliberate to follow the PSC type of process. I've actually had the deputy state services public services commissioner, Heather Baggett, on my interview panel as well. And so we've been very clear, as the minister says, to basically bring almost exactly the same rigor and process that the Public Services Commission would bring to it, just so that it can be plenty of public confidence in the appointment.

So New Zealanders can expect to see a fair reflection of people who represent those who have an interest in firearms and public safety on that group? I'm talking about the chief executive. Oh, chief executive. So I was meaning like the, once the actual panel itself, or the new body, the representatives who are appointed onto that group, can New Zealanders expect to see a fair representation of those who have an interest in firearms?

Yes. I'll just be clear, the comments I was making were in relation to the chief executive. Chief executive. And with respect to the members of the group, we would go through a process. We go through those sorts of processes across the board, and we provide advice to ministers that we give our best assessment against criteria that would cover off the sorts of things that you'd expect to see.

And who makes that final determination on who makes it onto that group? I think the decisions on that will be the minister's decisions at the end of the day on, as is always the case with these sorts of appointments. And that's Associate Justice or Justice who makes that call? It's Associate Justice. I would say it's probably, because it says in the legislation the minister in charge of firearms. But if there is a recommendation that appoints somebody that I may know, I will step out of that, as I have been doing throughout this term, and that

decision will fall probably onto the Minister of Justice. Thank you. Yeah, can I just observe, because I think Ian asked another question. In terms of the criteria that you referred to for the panel, who will determine that, and would that be in line with public service PSA guidelines?

The criteria is set out in the legislation of what the three panel members will need to have as criteria. One of them needs to be a lawyer with legal experience over five years. Another one would be a licensed firearm owner, and the other one will have some sort of regulatory experience and understanding in that realm, as well as having some knowledge on firearms.

Okay. All right, cool. You're right. I'm going to go to Mr. Todd Stevenson. Supplementary. To stay on the topic of arms, obviously that legislation's still before this actual committee, but money has been set aside in the budget. If the legislation passes, what will that money be used for? What are the next steps of, I suppose, operationalizing the Arms Act if passed?

Thank you. The $44.9 million that has been allocated is specifically for the implementation of the new Arms Act regime. So that will include the discussions that we've just had on the Firearms Licensing Review Committee to make sure that we set them up really well, as well as the separation of the regulator from the enforcer, and making sure that they are up and fit for purpose as well when they run. There's agreements that will be needed to be made with New Zealand Police on purchasing services from them to ensure

that there is no breakdown in services that are available because we are still having this vote under New Zealand Police, although it's managed by the Ministry of Justice. So we need to ensure that the flow is adequate between the new regulator and New Zealand Police. So there will be some discussions that will be had between the two about some of those things that need to be picked up And these costs will help pay for that.

Just a quick supplementary, Mr. Kibblewhite. So I assume a lot of that operational detail the minister's talking about- Mm... the planning is already underway and- Uh... the ministry's overseeing all that? We are. We've appointed an establishment chief executive who's with us today. Lukasz is sitting behind me here. So he is stepping that through. He's working both with the ministry and with police and with the current Firearm Safety Authority and just generally helping to shepherd that through.

So we are doing a, we've got effectively a comprehensive work program to set this up and establish it and have it ready to go. So just one last- Oh, may I just add to that, there's also a contingency within that $44.9 million that goes towards the courts to help pay for a judge. This is because we are introducing infringement notices, and we are upping the penalties as well as new offenses within the Arms Act.

So we're ensuring that the courts are able to be able to operate once we start enforcing some of these. Great. So subject to Parliament doing its work- Mm... you're confident that justice will be able to deliver this new Arms Act regime with all the steps you've taken? Well, we're certainly confident in the implementation process-... that we have underway at the moment.

There will be obviously a new entity created. They will take over that mantle. There'll be a close cooperation with police who will still be hosting that independent entity. And so potentially between the three agents, the new agency, the existing agency, and then police and the Ministry of Justice, yes, I'm confident that that's all on track.

Thank you. So before we get a new topic, anyone else want to ask about the Firearm Safety Authority? So we'll put that one to bed. Or do you want to... For example, I like to do them in groups. Right? They're different from safety education Well, no, we can go into that. If I may explain. Yeah. The current authority that sits within New Zealand Police is called the Firearm Safety Authority or Te Tari Pūreke.

Once the transfer occurs or we have royal assent of the bill, Firearm Safety Authority will then become Firearm Safety and Education New Zealand at that stage. There you go. Let's finish this topic, and then we'll move to new stuff. So, is there any sort of view on how that's going to operate?

So I know that when that shift happens, that there won't be police in there. But is there going to be an MOU or there'll be some institutional knowledge that'll be needed? Is there any more sort of detail on how the funding and all those sort of things work? Yeah. I'll give you high level, and if there's something else that Lukasz can add, then he definitely will. But, yes, look, sorry, what was the question?

So, how will it work in practice? Will there be an MOU setting out terms of agreement? Between the police. Yes. Is there a code of conduct for those affected and dealt with by police systems and HR, those housed within the Regulatory Authority or the Ministry of Justice, which has oversight over monitoring the regulator? All that sort of detail that we haven't seen- Yes... are we able to see any of that?

Yes, there will be agreements that will be made. We're actually working through a system of figuring out what actually needs to occur to make sure that both police have the information that they need, and the new regulator have these as well. There'll be new police liaison teams set up to work between New Zealand Police and the Firearm Safety and Education New Zealand as well.

And of course, we'll be implementing a red flag system, which will bring in other government agencies so that they can look to share information. So we don't have a situation like we had in 2019, where Brenton Tarrant was able to maneuver his way through our legislative system without government agencies being able to have any foresight or input. So we're trying to close those gaps there, and we will make sure that that is operational.

We will have firearms or arms officers working out of some police stations as well, so there'll be agreements that will need to be made between the new authority as well as New Zealand Police to make sure that they're still operational. Do you have anything to add to that, Lukasz? So at an agency to agency level, we'll have what's called a host agency agreement with police. So that sets out everything from real estate to desk space to corporate services and what it looks like.

And we are working through at the moment with the Association of the Employment Level, what that means for folks who are currently police employees who will be FSE staff in the future. And as the minister says, we're also working through the information sharing and data access agreements to support the right flow of information to improve public safety.

And is there any specific allocation within the budget specifically for IT? Because I know one of the fears has been that that real time intelligence for putting up red flags. Is there any additional funding in the budget to enable to make sure that the IT capability enables there to be very quick transfer of intel when required?

Yes, there is. And if you have a look at the budget lines, you'll see that there is an ICT tag contingency that has no money appropriated to it. There is money appropriated to it, but the reason why it doesn't show in the budget lines is for commercial sensitivities as they start to negotiate what those ICT upgrades will need to look like going into the future.

And is there any kind of indication about what that might be? ICT, we know can do lots of different things for government. Is there any kind of ballpark figure? I know there's been, is it 4.7? Is it within that 4.7 that's been allocated, or is that in addition to that? The $44.9 million that have been allocated to arms does not include anything to do with ICT, and it's not appropriate for me while the negotiations are taking place to even indicate what a figure may be at that stage. But by the time we get through that and we

come back next term, we'll be able to show you exactly what those figures are. But do you know what pot of money that would come from in the future, or that's unknown? The money has already been set aside in a tag contingency, so the money is sitting there. Right. I've got a supplementary from Rima Nakle. Supplementary today again, Minister and team.

Just with respect to the ICT normalization, Minister, how are we expecting that these improvements will benefit both public safety and service delivery? Well, what we have at the moment is ICT that is not working the way that it should do. So being able to upgrade it and having it fit for purpose is going to benefit not only the licensed firearm owners, but the Firearm Safety and Education New Zealand as they take over, and also, most importantly, New Zealand Police.

At the moment, there are three different ICT systems that do not speak to each other. So the first thing we need to do is be able to pull them all together, make sure that they're safe and secure, and make sure that the relevant access is available to those that need it. Mm. Thanks, Minister. Okay, let's go to a new topic. Mr.- Oh. Sorry, Mr. Chair, just before you move on to a new topic, I'd just like to express my thanks to the Justice Select Committee for their hard work on the firearms legislation. I understand it's been very constructive, and

I'm really pleased to hear that the committee has worked so well together, and I'd just like to take the opportunity to thank them for that. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Right. Scott, do you want anything, or have you got a new line of thinking? No. Not yet. Right. I'm going to go back to Honorable Jenny Anderson.

Thank you very much. How many FTEs are planned for the regulator, and how does that compare to the Firearm Safety Authority? The way that this is going to operate is on day one, and day one will be the day after royal assent. There will be no changes apart from those that are what I would call blue shirts. Members of New Zealand Police will be moving out of the Firearm Safety and Education.

I believe there's 12 at the moment that are going through a process with New Zealand Police to integrate back into other areas within Police. Everybody else will move over into the new entity. And why don't you want Police in there? Well, this is about rebuilding trust and confidence, but it's also about making sure that Police stick to their core business, which they do so well, and that's enforcement. So what we're doing is removing the regulatory and administration side of arms from New Zealand Police, giving it to this new entity, and allowing New Zealand Police to solely

concentrate on enforcement, which is their core business. I think many New Zealanders would think that they do trust New Zealand Police, particularly around their institutional knowledge of firearms. Why do you take a different position to that? And I think this is where it's really important that we still keep the police liaison team in place because they do have some knowledge there. But they're not the only ones with knowledge, and there is no reason why the Firearm Safety Authority in the future can't start bringing in others with knowledge.

Police are not the only people with knowledge about firearms, extensive knowledge, and I would argue that there are other and better experts within New Zealand that would be available. But don't you think it's kind of doing New Zealand a disservice that if there is someone who has good knowledge on firearms and they just so happen to be a police officer, wouldn't we want to draw upon that knowledge?

Sure. You would want to, and in some respects, they'll still be there. So to give you an example, new imports of new firearms will still be looked at at this stage by the team of New Zealand Police because they are the ones that already understand what it is that they're looking for. So we're not actually removing that part at this stage because they are the experts in that field. But I'm sure that they are not the only experts in that field. So it's not about just getting rid of something for the sake of it because public safety is still front of mind.

So they will still continue to have that team. But as I mentioned before, New Zealand Police are not the only experts, and I believe there are other experts out in the community that have more knowledge than some of them. What if someone leaves Police for a while and then comes? Is that okay then? Well, they're no longer employed by New Zealand Police- Right... which is a commitment that we made to the licensed firearm owners. I hear what you say about other New Zealanders, but there is a large portion of, I'll call them LFOs, within New

Zealand that need that trust and confidence to be rebuilt. And presumably, there'll be a lot of senior police staff who leave the police force, either because of age or other reasons, who are likely to want to be employed under the license as it was prior to 2019 or whenever the reforms were brought in.

Correct. Look, I understand at the moment that there are some that have actually left Police and are employed by Firearm Safety Authority, and they are quite welcome to move over. Yeah. Okay. What's the expected establishment date for the Firearm Safety and Education New Zealand new unit? We're hoping that we will get through legislation and have it go live, so to speak, around late September.

Late September. Thank you. And I think you might have answered this, but I'll just check. Will police staff currently performing firearms licensing functions for Te Tari Puraki, will they have the opportunity to transfer to the new, or they just don't, they don't? If they are signed up with New Zealand Police, they will not be transferring over, no.

And that's the employment issue you're dealing with at the moment in terms of police association. They'll figure out what happens. Yes. My understanding is that they will go back into the bigger police pool and will be reallocated new jobs. I'll go to Scott. Do one more or? One more, and then I'm going to go to Scott. On the authority's website, reported trust and confidence in the authority is at 65%. Has there been any modeling done to estimate an expected increase in trust and confidence with the separation of the

firearms regulation from police? Not to the best of our knowledge No, not yet. Not yet. Is that something you might think of doing? I think it's something that would be really important to do into the future, because we do need to see whether or not we are achieving what we want to. I'm hearing from the firearms community that some subtle changes that have been made already is helping to establish these new baselines. It's starting to work already, and I thank Rakesh, who's come in as acting chief executive, for some of the culture

change that we're starting to see and some of the improvements. So I think it would be a good idea to look at that into the future. But let's get established first. Right. Scott. Scott Willis. I may have missed an answer you've given to this, but what are the costs of the firearm safety ICT upgrades? What are we looking at?

You did miss it. We just went through and said that we won't be giving exact figures due to commercial sensitivity, and that is the reason why you see in the budget lines an ICT tag contingency with no figures to it. That's due to commercial sensitivities while the organization actually starts to look to employ or contract those to come in and do that work.

Okay. Any more questions on them? No. Right. Who else wants to ask a question? Oh, on a different topic. Yep. Let's go to a different-- I think we've done that, eh? Let's... And thank you for your assistance. Right. Todd. If I may say, Mr. Chair, I do have an open door policy and invite the member, if she has more questions, to come and talk to me about them.

I have made that open door policy to all opposition at any stage, and I'm willing to continue that. I did have one more, but- One more. Okay. Okay. It was on the ICT stuff. Will that ensure that police still have continued access to the firearms regulators information?

Do police get still full access to what's in there? Once we have that ICT set up, and we don't know what it's going to look like. So I had said to you that there are three different ICT workings that we have at the moment that don't speak to each other. One of them sits within police, and the other two sit outside of police. So we need to actually understand what the new ICT is going to look like to understand whether or not police and other agencies have direct access or whether or not they get the information through the regulator.

What is very important is that where police need to have real time access, they should continue to do so, especially in very quickly evolving situations. There is no intent whatsoever to leave police out of that, but what it's actually going to look like will depend on what the ICT solution looks like and whether or not it's direct access or whether it's by agreement, depending on what the ICT actually looks like once it's implemented.

Okay. Well, hopefully we're going with something like AML or something like that. Adoption, I was going to say. Adoption. Okay. Is that okay? Is that okay, Minister? I'm going to do adoption. Thank you. It was either that or AML, but I've decided to go with adoption. Yes, I want to obviously talk about what this budget sets aside for this new adoption. Again, it's legislation that's obviously before Parliament.

Obviously, the government, and again, want to acknowledge, obviously, the opposition stepped in last year, closed this quite serious loophole which could allow some various serious things to happen around adoption. Just what have you got planned going forward? How does this budget support your work in the adoption area so that we really do have a robust adoption regime and we are living up to good international standards?

Thank you for that question. The budget for what we're doing in the adoption system will come about from the main budget that has been allocated to the Ministry of Justice. But it is really important that we do make the changes that we have indicated. When we put through the temporary piece of legislation that stopped international adoptions from occurring under the current process, we did say that we would look to have a more firmer relevant piece of legislation that will ensure that the children that are coming in from overseas will be protected.

So there is now that replacement bill sitting with the Justice Select Committee, and we need to ensure that what goes through the court system, what goes through the DIA, as an example, and the Minister for Immigration is able to look after and protect those children.

So there are some minor changes that we're making in the way that the adoption of children will be progressed. But quite frankly, no matter what it costs, it should be paid for because looking after the children, especially children coming into New Zealand, we should be ensuring that their health and safety is maintained. More importantly, ensuring that those people, some of them with really bad criminal conviction lists, should not be able to get children in from overseas.

So the budget line is a part of the bigger Ministry of Justice budget allocation. But from my point of view, whatever it costs will be worth it if we save these children. And just a quick supplementary. And so Justice is leading this work, but then it's obviously got to coordinate with DIA, Immigration, and others.

Yes, they do. Just to make sure that it all works well and everybody is able to do their little bit, because some of the changes we're making is a little bit in Immigration, it's a little bit in the courts. We need to make sure that it all comes together. But Ministry of Justice will be paying for their bit in it.

Thank you. Can I-- I've got a supplementary from the honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. Just invite the minister to talk about the surrogacy work as well in that tranche before. I know there's a lot of people who are very interested to know what's happening in that space, to get some certainty around pretty personal arrangements.

Yes. Yes, the surrogacy, and I thank the committee once again for the work on the Surrogacy Bill, because it's been going for quite some time. The committee will know that it is a very complicated piece of legislation. It's only just been reported back. So at the moment, it's sitting on the order paper. It's not a piece of legislation that I'm going to be looking at between now and the end of term, purely because it is so complicated and we do have quite a large number or areas of work that we are trying to get finished

before the end of the term. We have less than three months, and I think the Surrogacy Bill actually needs more than a quick rush to try and get it through, especially when the committee has sat with it for so many years. I think it deserves better contemplation than a rush job. All right. Thank you. Right. Rima Nakley with another supplementary.

Yes. Supplementary, Minister, about the adoption work. As you know, and as I recall, swift work was done because from what I recall from your conversations with the House, is that there was information and evidence that was provided to you that was extremely alarming and disturbing, and you needed to act to protect children.

Minister, with the work that's being done, how are we going to be able to measure that protection is taking place? Well, I don't know whether or not it's a measure or perhaps a measure could end up being that we don't see these arrests of people. Mm.

What's really important is that we stop these people with criminal convictions actually adopting the children in the first place and bringing them through into New Zealand. So, I don't know if the team actually have measures in place for it. But I would've thought that actually not having the number of convictions and prosecutions that we're currently seeing will be a very good measure that we've got it into the right place.

I also feel that, if we can ensure that the children that are coming into New Zealand from overseas are coming from places where we have the same sort of controls, so for example, Hague Convention, the countries that have signed up to the Hague Convention, then we know that within their own home countries, they are applying really good, robust measures to ensure that children being adopted to overseas parents are being adopted to the right people.

We had a gap in our law, and that gap has been there for quite a few decades was my understanding. But some of the heinous type of stories that we were hearing, the prosecutions, the convictions that we were seeing most recently over a couple of years indicated to me as a mother of four, I cannot stand back and just let this continue as subsequent governments have. We needed to do something, and so I acted decisively and quickly.

And just a quick supplementary please. Minister, I know that you said that there was a gap. There is a gap. But maybe this is for the team as well. How did we miss the mark and allow so many people with very serious convictions to be able to adopt children?

Is it because of this gap? I think because of the way the system was designed and was set up and working, those cases weren't necessarily ones that the system had eyes on. Because they were coming through this route of inter-country adoption, it meant that many of the roles that other agencies would play in a domestic adoption weren't in play.

And so the way that the system worked allowed that to happen. And as minister said, they were showing up as sideways through people reporting more like labor exploitation and trafficking, and it was showing up in that space rather than through adoptions.

Through adoptions. Yeah. So adoption was being used, but it was showing up elsewhere. Mm. So, um. Thank you. It was joining the dots. Mm. There's a general comment there about the stewardship of legislation, so the Ministry of Justice is a department with over 150 acts, that we're looking after 50 different regulatory systems. And we're actually recognizing we need to be much more systematic at building a frame of how well all of that legislation is dealing with the challenges that it's designed for.

Yeah. And so Caroline and her team are going through a process, including building much stronger relationships with universities and practitioner communities across those different regulatory regimes. Sounds like a case of AI. Beautiful example of using AI, I'd imagine. Well, there'll be some opportunities for AI, but I'd caution from over-relying on it in that sort of situation.

Of course. All right. Yeah. Thank you. Hey, now, any more on the adoption bit? No. Right. We're happy with that, members? Right. Now we're going to go to a new topic- Yes... with Camilla Village. Um, Minister, you've introduced a bill, the Sale and Supply of Alcohol Amendment Bill, and I understand there'll be budgetary implications for the progress of that bill as it moves through the House.

And this is kind of related to that question about the joining up of legislation. One of the criticisms that we've heard about that bill is the fact that the bill, although it focuses on alcohol regulation, may be, in some instances, inconsistent with the object of the primary piece of legislation which it's amending, which is the Sale and Supply of Alcohol Act, and that's in relation to the focus on minimizing harm caused by alcohol consumption. So that's been a strong criticism that we've received on that legislation as we've been looking at

it, and I just wanted to give you the opportunity to respond to that. I have considered the Sale and Supply of Alcohol bill, with the justice team looking at some of the red tape around businesses and whether or not it's justified in the way that it is. We found that some of it was not, that in fact it was costing more money to business, including businesses not being able to guarantee that they could operate year to year.

Businesses being shut down with those wanting to object to renewals and those objections not even coming from within New Zealand or coming from within the same island. So while I wanted to ensure that the objectors still had the ability to object, they needed to be relevant to the area that they were objecting in because we were getting case studies showing that there were objections that were just coming in for the sake of it. So- I do not believe that I have done anything within the legislation that will create more alcohol

harm. I've tried to balance it in a way where it became fairer on the businesses that were trying to operate, but also allowed the community that were or are going to be impacted by alcohol sales to be able to still have their fair say. And this is why it's important to also let the community, or remind the community, that those that are out there helping people to do their objections, they're still available to help them. What we are suggesting is that those that can object should object from that territorial authority or within a kilometer of the

territorial authority's line. So I don't believe that we are creating more harm. I think we're creating more opportunity. We're doing some really good common sense things, like allowing zero alcohol within bars, which hasn't been potentially allowed in some cases. And I think that this is a good thing to get New Zealand moving without creating more harm.

Just a quick supplementary to this, and this might be more for the team that's assisting you. Many of the parts of that bill, I think it's arguable to say that there may lead to an increased either consumption in some instances, and that's in the specific allowance for alcohol to be served in areas where it wasn't previously. And also, that possibly the changes may result in fewer licenses, initial licenses or renewals being declined.

Is there evidence that greater consumption of alcohol, in a wider sense, leads to alcohol harm? What does the evidence say about that? I wonder if that's a question we might answer in writing to you, because there's a bit of nuance in there that it'll be good to get right. There's different ways in which you measure alcohol harm, whether it's at that population level.

And so there's particular measures where you look at harm at a population level and in these particular demographic groups in the way that you can measure harm. So it's not a simple answer, and I would rather answer that very accurately, if that's okay. Okay. Yeah. What I can point out, though, is that between October 2025 and February 2026, we had 1,000 less incidents of victims involved in alcohol-related violence.

So we're seeing the trend of consumption coming down and the trend of violence also coming down. Right. Todd Stevenson's got a- Just a supplementary just on the bill, just kind of line of questioning. In the bill, isn't there some areas like same-day delivery where actually it's introducing a regulatory regime that doesn't exist today and actually strengthening protections?

Correct. Some of the concerns that we had heard from the public was around those same-day deliveries or rapid deliveries of alcohol. So we wanted to ensure that we don't just have people drunk getting more alcohol very, very quickly. So we have included within the bill the fact that a delivery driver must not deliver to somebody who's already intoxicated, and that they must ensure that the person is over the age of 18. There are some companies, and I won't name them because I'm not sure if I can, but some companies that are actually already doing this.

And one of the things that I've heard from them, from two companies in particular, is that if they do turn up to a home to deliver alcohol and somebody is drunk or they're not 18 years old, that they can remove that or not deliver it, so to speak, and take it back to the bottle store and they'll still be paid.

So no one's missing out there except for maybe the underage drunk. Right. We've got one last topic, AML, and Mr. Carl Bates is going to put you on the raft for that. Thanks, Chair. And thanks for the work around AML and the removal of red tape that that is going to achieve.

What is there covered in this budget to allow for the rollout of the legislation that's been going through the house? Yes. Well, the AML legislative changes that we're making are quite huge. They're really big. You won't see a specific byline for AML because, again, it will come under the justice budget. But what we've done is three pieces of legislation have already gone through, and we have one more that's still coming, and that will be quite substantial. But we're also looking at how we're going to fund it.

So we have already looked at a national strategy. We are working in partnership with everybody that's involved with this as well to ensure that when we do have this national strategy levy regime come into force, that everybody's happy with how it's actually operating.

We don't want to be doing things to business. We need to work with business. And with AML, we all know that things evolve very, very quickly. We have different ways of being able to launder money. And making sure that everybody is working together in a realm that is effective for them is going to be very, very important as we move through these regulatory changes.

So the policy work that you've got coming that's covered under this budget period, is that going to further reduce the compliance impact on business across New Zealand? Yes, that's right. I am currently working on a fourth bill. Hopefully, I will have this finished and ready to be introduced before the end of the term. But as I earlier mentioned, the justice team have one of the highest amount of work- Yes... so you'll know this- We know.

... Justice Committee, going through. So actually trying to make sure that they can get that work done so I can have it introduced. But we have already put through three pieces of legislation, which is a really good start. Not only that, it means that we will look a lot better when we are evaluated with the Financial Action Task Force looking to get that mutual evaluation underway next year.

We've made some really good changes, mitigating some risk, again, removing unnecessary red tape. And what's most important is for the man on the street- Being able to understand this compliance is actually there to keep them safe, not to just box tick, which is currently what's happening over and over again for some people.

Thank you. Well, that is a glorious conclusion. So I want to thank the minister. I want to thank everyone who's come this afternoon. We are about to hear from the next minister, so Bella, I'd like to thank you, Minister- Thank you very much, Eddie... for your start.
You've got Helen Clark coming in, but I see Dan's- Helen White. Helen White, did you say? Helen White. What happened to you, Minister?
Minister,

lovely to have you with us this afternoon. I've got to say that you are last but not least. Some of your colleagues beside you, we've seen before. Some repeatedly. Some can't stay out of this room. But anyway, it's nice that you're all here. So we've got 45 minutes.

Yeah. Obviously, you'll do a short introduction, then we'll get into questions. Yeah. Thank you. So, it's always a privilege to be able to come and speak to the work across government in a space that often isn't spoken about enough. The conversation around family harm and sexual violence is often a hidden problem in our country, and the more that we can bring this issue into the forefront of New Zealanders, the better. So beside me is Andrew Kibblewhite, the Secretary for Justice and Chair of the Interagency Executive Board,

and Cam Shirley, the Acting Chief Executive of the center. I also have officials from New Zealand Police, MSD, Corrections, Oranga Tamariki, ACC, and the Social Investment Agency, representing the core agency partners for the board. So if there are any questions that you would like to have around those agencies, there is someone here to be able to answer those questions.

I'm responsible for the Centre for Prevention of Family and Sexual Violence and the Interagency Executive Board. The vote amounts to roughly $20 million per annum. 20 minutes, Minister. What I can't do as the minister is direct how board agencies spend their money. I can set expectations on them to work together with a system view and to consider implications of operational decisions, and I'm strengthening-... those accountabilities through development of key performance indicators moving forward.

That's time. From the vote- Ma'am, stop... of about 20 million per annum, approximately 20% goes towards community organizations. About $4.3 million in '26 to '27. Much of that is the involvement in the multi-agency responses to family violence. The center's activities support the board and agencies to coordinate the all of government approach.

The latest version of Te Aorerekura identifies the system shifts that were needed and how they need to be achieved through successive action plans. This is not a short-term plan. This is a long-term plan, and it doesn't matter which government is in place. I'm sure that everybody across this table wants to see better outcomes and breaking that cycle of harm.

So that's my biggest focus. The current action plan has prioritized three focus areas: investing in commissioned services, keeping people safe through strengthened multi-agency responses, and stopping violence. Each of these requires a sustained focus from successive governments building on each step to achieve the change.

I'm also concerned about the increasing complexity of sexual violence, and I know that a well-trained and capable workforce is critical to caring for survivors and preventing re-offending. I think I'll leave it open to questions because I think it's more important to answer the questions than go through this in depth.

Nice. But what I would say is, it has been an absolute privilege for myself over the last two and a half years to go up and down the country, speak to organizations that may not necessarily have ever had a voice at the table, speak to victims of violence, victims of sexual assault, and hear their experiences of the system. And being able to have that opportunity to make those changes is an absolute privilege.

Thank you, Minister. We've got some new members. I would ask that you ask questions in the context of the budget. Mm-hmm. I'm going to start off with Helen White Thank you. We have a real phenomenon out there at the moment in sexual violence, and that's the strangulation issue.

Mm. When I talk to people who are in this sector, they tell me that very few people engaging in this behavior know how harmful it is- Mm... and what injury it is doing. What are you doing in the funding this year to focus on what is a really scary phenomenon in terms of educating people as to the dangers of that practice?

Yeah. Education is really important, and I absolutely agree that strangulation has been brought front and center because of the law change, which made that a specific focus in legislation around strangulation, because the harm that is caused by that is just unacceptable. I also had conversations with MEDSAC, whose job it is to show up and actually take the evidence around strangulation, and making sure that we have a plan moving forward, that we have enough resources for MEDSAC to be able to actually get in front of these victims.

So there's two areas. There's crisis, when we actually need somebody there, when somebody's come forward with strangulation, and there's also education. So I will hand over to Cam to have a conversation around a plan on keeping people safe, and what we are currently doing across agencies, and if there's any other agency that wants to speak to this in the form of education.

Perhaps just addressing sexual violence to start with. We know this is a really complex space, and it's moving very fast in terms of some of the behaviors and some of the means that we're starting to see emerge. Minister's been clear with us, based on our own advice as well, that sexual violence needs elevation in terms of our broader priorities. So the Minister outlined the three initial priorities, investing in commissioning well, keeping people safe through multi-agency responses, and stopping violence.

We are over the next year just preparing some advice around what an increased focus in sexual violence might look like, as well as workforce capability, which is a really important part of that as well. We've recently released and published some sexual violence capability frameworks, which really set the standards for both organizations and workforces to ensure that we are serving people as we need to and providing the necessary supports out there.

I'm not hearing anything concrete. Can you tell me something that is going on that is concrete, because it's so desperate? Thanks, Minister. Thank you. I just want to acknowledge, you actually answered this question or a similar question last time when we were in here, and I really appreciated your candidness about the seriousness of the issue. So I'm very interested in your participation.

Oh, thank you. I feel like a frequent flyer, I think. Yeah. So firstly, you're right. It is a serious issue, and actually this is not a new issue. That's the first thing, with family harm. Since the legislation has come into being, what it's actually enabled us to do is actually action it and call it for what it is.

So we've really welcomed that, which is why there was actually so much lobbying to get it through. What we've done is, if we talk about it from just a response rate, and the amount of family harm investigations that we carry out each year is significant. So we have trained all our staff in relation to strangulation. And of the significant number of family harm events and investigations that we do every year, this is one of the key areas that we're looking for.

I think to your point, and you are very well-versed in this, we know that we have to identify, we have to interview the victims, we have to ask them specific questions about strangulation because it is such a warning flag for more serious offending, i.e. could lead to homicide. So it is something that we are actively doing in our response. It is something that we are actively doing in our investigations.

And yeah, I'm happy to answer any other questions. Probably what I would say, though, it's also a bookend, because strangulation is part of a far wider remit for the family harm scenario, even if we go back to just the stalking and harassment that was brought in recently. So the more tools we have, the more legislation we have to enact it, that's actually a good thing.

But the training and the training framework that has been laid out from the center, I'll speak on behalf of our agency, but we have literally trained over 10,000 people, and we keep doing that in our family harm courses. And so strangulation is significant for all of those courses for our frontline. And it's also about supporting the community organizations in that safety framework response when you do have somebody coming through your door that has been through, whether it be strangulation, sexual assault, rape, that they have

an appropriate response to that and can be able to speak to exactly what you're talking to. What is the harm that's been caused? What medical advice have you had, considering the harm that it might cause medically? And who they can put that person in front of. So it's about also training the response of community and agencies. So it's not just about training government, it's about training agencies in how to give the right information to those people who are walking through their door who have suffered from strangulation.

So, Minister, part of the conversation we had last time was about the blurring of the lines. That there are young women in particular consenting to this behavior, and in fact, the harm it's doing causes things like dementia. And that there is very little knowledge in young men or young women, and probably older ones at this point, about the dangers of what they're doing. What educational tools are we using in this space?

For example, I have suggested to you, Minister, that we look at ACC doing a rollout on this and educating. What are we putting in resource-wise in this budget to education in this space that's wide, that shocks people into understanding the harm that is being done.

There are many programs across many agencies, where they are focusing on education in those spaces. I'm not over all the education programs, but there may be someone from ACC in here that can speak to some work that they've been doing. It might not necessarily be in the specific strangulation space, but in the education space around- Do you want to add something?

... harm and how to respond to harm. If you want to say something, just come on up. Yeah. Kia ora. No specific education programs on specifically strangulation. But what we have recently done is released a campaign called Little Matters, which is a toolkit for parents and providers, that gives them tools and strategies so that they can incorporate them in their day-to-day practical lives to prevent child sexual abuse. So that's one of the initiatives that we've recently published.

Okay. In terms of one education favorite. One education favorite. I might move onto that issue that you're talking about. Yeah. Given you've given me the in on it, but I am concerned that strangulation itself, it needs a program around this, and I'd urge- Mm-hmm... the minister to consider getting one. Can I? Yes. As the board, we will be bringing advice in relation to what we're doing across sexual violence. The minister's been clear that she expects us in the next iteration of the action plan to be putting a stronger focus

on sexual violence. Yes. So as we bring that advice up, we'll definitely be looking at strangulation as Fantastic. Before you change, I just want to make sure we finish this topic, right? Mm. So just, Assistant Commissioner, you said with the change of the law, it gave the police the opportunity to charge people to- Mm... call it for what it was. What I was just keen to do is get a bit of feedback in terms of what that's meant from police prosecutions or the way you're going about dealing with offenders.

I'm trying to get empirical evidence what it has meant. So on that, actually, I've just asked for some numbers, so we can get back to you. Apologies- Okay... on the actual prosecution numbers. But look, what it actually means is, because we know and you all know, it is such a risk. It's a flag. And so what it's enabled us to do is actually go and investigate, look for the total evidence, talk about what's actually happened, because family violence encompasses so much.

Yeah. And within the realms of family violence, if we want to break it down, you have child abuse, sexual child abuse, you have rapes, you have significant serious injuries. And whilst we're working really hard in the multi-agency space, because we're bookending it, right? In fact, in my 35 years, more than I've actually ever seen before, of us coming together and working together to bookend it, to actually prevent it, to reduce the serious incidents, to have that harm de-escalation. But the reality is, it's still happening across this

country way too much. So what it enables frontline responders- Mm... to do, whether we are constables or investigators, is to go in and look at the totality and to work really hard on that investigation. And what it's enabled us to do is where there is strangulation, we can actually charge for it in the appropriateness. So that's where we're sitting.

And sir, I can get you the numbers later on this afternoon if you want me to. All right. If you can do that, that would be great. Just any more questions on strangulation before Helen goes onto something new? Right. Okay, Helen, let's- Great. I want to go to that space. It probably is, whoever can stay at the table, work it out. Yeah, just stay there, guys.

The issue arose of child abuse, and we're seeing this new phenomenon. The internet is so dangerous. One of the things that I have been particularly concerned about is the ordering up of child abuse overseas. And there is basically now a market for this stuff.

500,000 Filipino children last year were abused. As my understanding is, that's 500,000 where people in other countries like New Zealand have been ordering up that abuse, and it has been happening, and they've been paying for it on the internet. And we have also children in New Zealand exposed to things that are absolutely horrifying.

Mm. What is going on in that space, given that this is violence, this is sexual violence, and prevention of it is the name of the game here? The member has alighted on two of the issues that the board has been particularly focused on this year, and that we're thinking about in the context of the next iteration of the action plan, the sexual violence more generally and the online harm.

So with both of those, what the center has done is prepared an overview of current programs and of the stats as we understand them. And we're now going through the process as we think about the next iteration of the action plan, about what we would put in that. We haven't yet finalized what will be in the next version of the action plan. But again, the online harm, and particularly for children, is something that would feature in that Yes. You look like you've got an answer. No?

I don't know whether I've got an answer. I've definitely got an opinion on it. So child exploitation online has been something that, actually, internationally and globally, we have been trying to confront for well over a decade. And so the first thing, to your point, this is not a New Zealand issue. This is global child exploitation, which takes many forms, actually. And the more we go online, the more the exploitation can be.

So, for example, with police, we've had our online child exploitation team, has been going for well over a decade. I can't give you it. And our staff there do a fantastic job, as do DIA, and that is why it's so imperative that we link, and there is a global network of enforcement and agencies who are grappling this.

The AI ability and the IT technology to actually identify these images that can go all around the world, the investigation technique to actually identify what country they're in, sometimes what village they're in, and then to have the response is significant. And it's a growing presence. But I probably just want to say, this is probably an area, if we're talking child exploitation online, that we actually have been working and building our skills with, not only here, but across the world.

Yep. Yeah. And what, I guess, if we're going to be speaking to moving forward, that will always be something that is invested in. But if we speak to a realistic thing that I've actually feel like we've made a real difference and a change, and it may not just be in the online space, but it's in the space of protecting children who may be trafficked around the country.

You'll notice the changes that we did in the adoption space, where we saw that there was a gap in the adoptions legislation that allowed children to be moved around the world using our Adoptions Act, to enable that to happen. So when I'm seeing things that, in New Zealand, where I can make a real change and know that it's going to protect children, I will advocate in that space.

But as Tasha said, this has been a long-term issue that we'll be constantly invested in. What I would be concerned about, and it's good work, and it's amazing that people even can handle doing that work. It's so traumatic. But that's all ambulance at the bottom of the cliff. What are we doing to stop that material even occurring? Do we need to start to come down hard on the technologies, given that those tools also allow for this material to be identified and prevented from being created? Basically, there's a market,

and we're allowing that market to occur. Mr. Chair, can I just be really clear that the center actually, within this budget, isn't responsible for that specific funding. We can put together a plan and a work program, which I'm responsible for, is leading the direction of the areas that we would like agencies to be focusing on. But there isn't anything in the budget specifically for technologies in that space.

I'm clear on that, Minister. I just want to make- It's about what you're doing with the advice. Yeah I know you might be clear. But I just want to make sure that the people listening- Yeah... are clear that it doesn't fall in the budget. I'm glad you've done that clarification. We'll just have this one response. Yeah. I think, Assistant Commissioner. I think the key thing is, just because an issue has been around a long period of time, I would hate anyone to think that anyone was apathetic to it.

And so to your point, it is a challenge getting things taken offline when online is so visible. But even today, our police commissioner is in London. He spent the day meeting with Google and other platforms. We are constantly, and it's not just police, but from an enforcement perspective, we're making sure that we've got the right international partnerships to be having the right conversations.

Because actually, this is an all of us issue. And so, we are doing that. We are actively doing that across New Zealand and global, particularly around the child exploitation. But, yeah. Right. Now I'm going to go to the Honorable Marama Davidson. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Kia ora, Minister. Kia ora. And all of the team.

Do continue. I really took on board your genuine concern at the opening of your kōrero. I know how much this means to you. I know how much this work means to you, and I've always been really clear about that. I also know how much this work means to the departments and agencies across Parliament, across government.

And that's why I'm quite concerned that there hasn't been, over the three years of this government, any additional funding, any new funding, in any of the '20, '24, '25, or '26 budget. It has become clear that even at the moment, there is a proposal to cut 26 key roles at the center.

The center which ultimately was to provide an overview and a level of accountability distinct for violence prevention that was not in existence beforehand. And so I'm wanting to ask the minister how is she able to reconcile this work as a priority when she has received no additional funding in any of this government's budget, but in fact has faced funding cuts from previously allocated funding to this really crucial work?

And I understand and really respect the question. But what I would say is, the review on the center is not about saving money or cutting back on this important work. The process was commissioned by the executive board, and it was not about reducing FTEs, or meeting a target or baseline savings. So I just want to make that very clear.

This was about ensuring that the board and the center are focused on the system being effective, well coordinated, and delivering an impact in our communities. We've known for a while that there is not enough accountability across agencies when it comes to this space.

Absolutely. I think we've had conversations- Exactly... in meetings together around this, and how do we create better accountability. I think the center sometimes became a distraction, where they were the focus and other agencies would assume that the center would take care of this stuff. And that was not, from my understanding, the intent of the center. The center was there to support agencies into making informed decisions around where they move forward. And the board was created so that you could have an around table of all the executives that have a role

to play in this space, talking to each other, and making sure that when they were making decisions in this space, that they were making it as a whole of the systems approach rather than a siloed approach. Unfortunately, the center's scope widened, and there was a bit of a confusion about what was the purpose of the center.

This came from the sector itself, up and down the country, having conversations with the sector, not really understanding what the center's role was. So no final decisions have been made around what the center's going to look like, but the discussion is how can we make the center as efficient as possible to support agencies to make really informed decisions and choices. So over the last two years, the center has done some pretty amazing work at building the foundation of what we currently have.

So when I say that, I mean we did not have a very good view of where our money was going, and where we were spending it, and whether we were having an effective outcome in our communities. The center has been able to draw together the data from across the whole of government, to have a real clear understanding of where our direct spend and family harm and sexual violence is going. We are able to now break that down into sexual violence or family harm spend.

We are able to break that down now into regions. What is our spend in the regions, and what kind of outcome are we having in those regions? Now that we have that clear framework of where we have started, we can make really informed decisions moving forward. In regards to contracts, I have not been made aware, and you can correct me if I'm wrong, of a single agency that has had a current contract stopped.

What I've been made aware of is there were a lot of contracts that had time limited funding, that came to an end, and a decision had to be made by agencies whether that time limited funding was going to become business as usual, or whether they were going to redirect their focus into a different space. And that is the current process that is happening.

So I think we have to be very clear rather than just saying cuts, that time limited funding is not a guarantee for future funding, and we have to make really informed decisions. Thank you, Minister. Were you going to say something, Mr. Kibble? If I might, I want to-- A couple of points. The first issue on the funding issues.

Mm-hmm. As far as the center goes, we've actually looked to protect the center from the specific funding cuts- Mm-hmm... that other agencies- Okay... have experienced. And even the change proposal that's underway at the moment, it does generate some savings, but those savings are not an obligation from outside, and they will stay within the center. So they will give more leverage to the board to be able to deploy those funds. So the other part of the funding, as the minister sets out, the center's done a lot of really good work at understanding

the landscape of where the funding's gone. It's working in closely with the Social Investment Agency across those areas of government activity. Family violence and sexual violence is one of the things that gets taken into account. So there hasn't been big increases specifically coming for family and sexual violence. Mm-hmm. But that's absolutely been taken into account when resource allocations have been looked at across the board.

Understood. Mm-hmm. And within the center, we've actually looked to protect the resources around the center from the small kind of incremental cuts that most agencies, including the Ministry of Justice, have had to deal with. As far as the change proposal, the minister has described it well. It is a change proposal that's underway at the moment, and it isn't a reflection on the quality of the people in there or the caliber of the work that they have done. I just would really acknowledge that.

I think the center has a strong Record of achievement. But when the board did the review, the review actually reaffirmed, the first thing it says is if we didn't have something like this, we would need to create something like this because of the need for agencies across government to work more effectively together.

But it went on to say, as the minister has said, that we have lost some of the focus around those core functions, which are fundamentally the same as when the board was established in the first place. We need a board that the agencies can hold each other to account and do that with quite a sharp focus.

We need a board that can be giving quite strategic advice, such as we talked about already in sexual violence and online harm. So there is a sort of analytical strategic brain kind of function that we need there, and we really need to be strengthening the work of the other agencies to do their line delivery. So that's very much the intent of the review.

As I say, it's out for consultation at the moment. The staff there and others are giving feedback on it, and we'll have to reflect on that. Thank you. Yeah, keep going. I appreciate that. Thank you. Staying with efficacy and of investment and resources, which of course are precious, but in light of pressures on other agencies, the very agencies that you have an overview across, and in light of what we're hearing from across the country and community and frontline organizations who are fearing funding cuts, not the least respected in Wellington, sexual violence

prevention and specialists, the only one in the Wellington region, and one of the few in the country. Can the minister, however, in light of a really constrained fiscal environment, what assurances can the minister give that we can ensure that the center or the work more broadly has the capability across its Māori, Pacific, and ethnic workforces?

In particular, I believe your officials were talking about the monitoring and reporting system performance, and for some people, that might not feel like frontline impacts, but it's crucial for efficacy. Can the minister give some assurances that those sort of guarantees across the work will be able to be upheld?

Yeah, and that's why one of the focuses was really around workforce capacity and capability, and actually encouraging agencies to support community to build up their capacity and capabilities, because that's so important that community feels like they are supported in that space. There are many areas that we are doing that.

We're doing that in the form of microcredentials. I'm actually off this afternoon to celebrate one of the first graduations of a Pasifika microcredential in Auckland, because we need to make sure that where we see gaps, where agencies don't feel so confident in certain spaces, especially sexual violence.

It's raised with me up and down the country that some agencies are very confident in the family harm space, but when it comes to sexual violence, they will provide services, but the confidence isn't as high. So in my role, everything that I do, I would expect agencies, when they're making changes around the family violence, sexual violence space, where we are pivoting and prioritizing our spend, that they are bringing those changes to the board for discussion first around the board, and Andrew Pillowit can probably speak to that more.

And my role is not about specifically making decisions for agencies. It's about advocating to make sure that every decision, when funding discussions are made, are done with te ao māori kura and the focus areas of the second action plan in mind, and definitely, it's around strengthening that workforce across agencies and community.

Okay, cool. Let's move on. More questions? Is there another question? One more little one. Yeah, you go for it. All right. And then I'll go to supplementary. Thank you. Appreciate that. Thank you. Is the minister confident that this government really does continue to prioritize violence prevention? Absolutely. In the budget. In this budget, absolutely. In the budget for the center, the focus is now around now that we have this information, now that we have a real good understanding, even region by region. And I think it's really important to

express that we now know our prevention spend and our crisis spend. We now know our family harm spend and our sexual violence spend. And if we have a good understanding in our regions of what the areas of harm are, we can refocus and reprioritize to make sure we're having a really focused decision making a region by region rather than just trying to guess.

Right. So Camilla Billif. Thank you. Yeah, just on the Change program in the Centre for Family Violence or Sexual Violence. Don't you think it's pretty cold comfort to those about approximately one-third of the staff there who are facing losing their positions to hear that they've been protected from other government cuts? And bearing in mind that some of these people are specialists who do deal with quite significant matters in the family and sexual violence matter.

There's no doubt that change processes, especially when jobs are in question, are difficult and traumatic. So it probably is very cold comfort to staff to hear me say that the budget has been protected, yet we are looking to make some savings in there. What I would say about the staff is that they're really committed to this kaupapa.

I've had a range of feedback, but very little of it is saying that there wasn't some opportunity to sharpen up the way the center functions and how it's working, but I think the staff are processing that at the moment, and we'll get quite a range of views through this consultation process.

Just finally, is it really realistic though to say that there's not going to be an impact on the work that is done if such a significant number of roles are going to be potentially at risk? Well, the challenge through this process is for us to understand that we will be creating a little bit of additional resource that can be deployed in other spaces.

And so that other deployment will generate opportunities to do other things that we're not currently doing. So on balance, the board's view is that there is opportunity to tighten up the range of work that we do out of the center, bring it back to its core initial purpose, which is around holding the agencies to account.

It's around helping chart the direction of where we go from here, and it's about supporting the line agencies, often through accountability and clarity, to do the work that they have to do. As opposed to, as the minister said, sometimes I think the center may have looked to compensate for other agencies, and I don't think that's actually helping the system as a whole function better.

We're still having that conversation. I'm going into it with, obviously, we've done some work. We've done a review. We've been very transparent with the team about what that looks like, and there will be feedback on it. So I'm open to receiving that feedback and coming up with arrangements that are as effective as possible. Right. Mr. Todd Stevenson going to take us into new territory.

I am, new territory. And the minister, in your opening, you talked about developing KPIs, key performance indicators. Mm. Does that relate to the center, the board, both? What do you see happening in that space? And maybe kind of flies a little bit on top what Mr. Kimber White was saying, because I'm assuming that the board, Mr. Kimber White, has its own set of KPIs because you do want to obviously hold the individual agencies jointly responsible for delivering things.

But maybe just talk a bit more about that because I'm interested in what they might look like. So it's about probably both. Yeah. But with the board, I think for a long time, there's been a discussion around accountability for the board, and making sure that they are doing what was intended. And sometimes things can happen where maybe it hasn't met the whole of board's expectations, and there has to be some KPIs set in that space. So that's what I'm working through at the moment, around how do we put KPIs in place. It may

not necessarily be myself putting those KPIs in place. It might be the minister in charge of those agencies setting a KPI specifically for those board members. And obviously, is the idea of that to try and drive this joint responsibility? Yes. And again, and maybe Mr. Kimber White- Mm... kind of comment from your perspective after the minister about- Yeah... how you view it, because obviously you're leading the board.

Yeah. Ministers are very clear across all these agencies that the siloing has to stop. But we also have the conversation with the community is the same. But we can't sit here as ministers and tell the community to stop siloing and to work together if we're not leading by example in government.

So I often say changing legislation and regulation in this space is the easy part, but changing practice and culture- Mm... can be quite difficult. So it's about setting clear expectations about how we want to work moving forward, which is as a system as a whole, rather than individual siloed decision making.

So there will be room for KPIs to set those accountabilities. Yeah. Right. I've got a- I'd say measuring joined up-ness is notoriously hard. Yeah. You don't want to just measure meetings or meeting attendance or anything like that. Heaven forbid, hey?

Heaven forbid. So I think it's actually a really hard question, but I'd answer it at several levels. One is that we do publish overall outcome measures, and we've just published in the last probably month, and that was for the preceding 18 months, and it's going to be every two years we will keep publishing that. So that frames the overall performance in family violence and sexual violence, and that tells a really interesting story.

So that gives us some comfort that some things are moving in the right direction. It also identifies some areas where progress is proving challenging. But to say that all comes to the performance of the board is a bit of a long bow. The next level is at the action plan level, and that's where this minister and her predecessor have been clear that you need action plans, and that those action plans should track what we collectively hold each other to account for.

And we've actually done really well against the action plan that has been in place and we're coming to the end of the action plan under Minister Choate's leadership. And so part of the work that we're doing at the moment is saying, well, because it's designed to be essentially rolling action plans, what should the next iteration of that action plan look like?

And that goes to some of the conversation we've already had and some of the areas we think we will shift the focus to. But so at that point, we'll then be looking and holding, as we have with the last action plan, how are we delivering against that. And that's probably our best sense of what we're doing collectively, because that doesn't in any way capture all of the activity that agencies do, but it captures the things that we identify are most important to be able to give a cohesive, coherent effort. And so that would be the primary way

that we would hold each other to account. Okay, I've got a quick supplementary from Rima Nakha, and then I'm going to finish with Helen White with a new line of questioning. Supplementary, thank you. Welcome, Minister. Notwithstanding the name Elimination of Family and Sexual Violence, would you say at this stage there is a clarity of purpose from the board and everyone involved, or are these actions like setting these new KPIs and the action plan helping to achieve more of a clarity of purpose in terms of how we're going to reach
