the elimination of- So I was very clear when I first came into this role that elimination is an excellent goal to try and have, but it's a long way away. And so with the second action plan, the focus, and you'll see the title of the second action plan, is actually breaking the cycle of violence.

Mm. Because with each and every person that we get in front of and we help, we are breaking that cycle of violence, which will lead to eventually, hopefully, a close to eliminating the harm that's going on in our communities. But the ultimate goal that we focus on is breaking that cycle of harm with everything that we're doing across agencies.

And every service we provide, every service that we're commissioning, the focus needs to be around does it fit into the seven focus areas of the Te Aorere Kura action plan? And are we making sure that we're measuring the outcomes of that investment? Which is why the social investment agency work has been so important.

Okay, now Helen White's going to bring us home. Thank you. Project Whetū was something that was canceled. $5 million has been returned. It's an incredibly important idea to centralize and to make sure that there's that kind of information sharing. We're back to manual processes, as I understand it, which are pretty seriously risky.

Can you tell me where are we at with a replacement for this, and what's happening in terms of funding that kind of work? Ultimately, this conversation was had with the board because it's all of systems approach. And I wasn't given the assurances from the board that they would support moving forward with Project Whetū. So it was agreed that now was not the right time to progress with this technology project, and it will be reviewed and revisited in the future. So what I would say though is, is all the work that has been

done is there, it won't be lost. But moving forward with a massive technology project, I wasn't given the assurances that I would be supported in doing that. I'm advised that the total spend of Project Whetū over the past two years has been around $2 million. However, this resulted in not just work on the technology project, but the non-technical aspects of Project Whetū are now being worked on and rolled out.

So, it's not all stopped, but there will be conversations in the future about actually being able to fund a major technology project. Right. But I'll hand over to Ken or- Maybe for Helen still. Yep I'll just go. Look, this is, I agree with you, a really important piece of the puzzle when you're wanting to work around families, individuals, children and harm.

You want to be able to connect information up from the different agencies that are working on it. It's why the multi-agency tables are an important piece of the puzzle. The technology bit of Project Whetū was to provide- Mm... kind of a technology underpinning for that. We are not without any underpinning at the moment. We're still using the police, FSS, the family safety system, and that still is functioning and we're grateful to police for keeping that moving.

But it's not a nationally consistent or a standard process. So the idea will return for Project Whetū. The work we've done thus far has actually put us in a much better position to keep building out the systems in the various locations where we are working on the multi-agency responses. There's a lot of work going on in the digital space across government. I think as that starts to settle, there will absolutely be opportunity for us to then ask whether any of the different information sharing or case management systems that are existing in some of the

core agencies, which could be police, it could be MSD, it could be Oranga Tamariki, whether some of those systems can be adapted to give us what we need in the Project Whetū space. So, as a board, at the end of the day, we did not have- Nice... enough of an alignment and clarity around hosting and an ability to push the project forward at this point.

So I would say it's a pushing pause rather than a stopping forever. But for now, we're progressing by making sure we get the online work- Time for one more question... and location work functioning properly. I think we're going to have this last question, right? I think we've had a good answer. Yeah, it's just to say, it has always worried me that we haven't got these systems across the children's sector anyway.

Have you looked at the possibility of connecting it up with risk systems for children in particular? Can I? I'll just speak to the children's system, because the children's technology system is very old, very clunky. It's a system called SIRAS. And Oranga Tamariki has put in a massive investment into upgrading SIRAS to make sure that we can actually have information on hand about young people, and their needs, what needs have been met and haven't been met at the click of a button. So there's not no technology upgrades going on across the system.

The children's system is getting an upgrade, but it might not necessarily be Project Whetū. But there are upgrades happening. All right. I'm going to call it quits at that point. Thank you, Minister, for attending today. Thank you for your department or your center, I should say.
And, Mr. Kimber, I hope enjoy your quiet whiskey tonight or whatever you drink. But hopefully- You can go home now maybe... you can go home.
